meta.title: Stellar — AI Recipe App
meta.subtitle: AI-powered mobile app that turns whatever's in your fridge into a recipe

data.headline: The kitchen shouldn't require a culinary degree.
data.body: Stellar is an AI-powered recipe app designed to make cooking feel less like a chore and more like a creative act. As sole designer, I led the full UX process — from research and empathy mapping to final UI — with a single goal: make meal planning accessible to anyone, regardless of skill or time.

creative_direction.notes: Blur-in for the problem headline — it's a revelation. The real problem wasn't what you expected.

data.headline: People don't have a recipe problem. They have a decision problem.
data.body:
There are more recipes online than anyone could cook in a lifetime. The problem isn't access — it's the daily 6pm question of 'what do I actually make tonight?' with a half-empty fridge, 30 minutes, and decision fatigue from the rest of the day.

Existing apps made it worse. More filters, more browsing, more scrolling. The opportunity wasn't to add more recipes — it was to take the decision off the user entirely.

creative_direction.notes: Slides from left — text leads, data follows. Research section should feel like evidence arriving.

data.headline: Investigating habits, frustrations, and attitudes toward AI in the kitchen.
data.body: 20+ users surveyed, 6 interviewed in depth. Methods: quantitative online survey, empathy mapping, Jobs to Be Done framework. The goal wasn't to validate assumptions — it was to find where cooking actually breaks down for real people.
data.image_hint: Empathy map or affinity board from interview synthesis — clusters around 'decision fatigue', 'fridge contents', 'trust in AI suggestions'

creative_direction.notes: Numbers do the talking here — drop them in cleanly after the research narrative. Fast stagger, feels like data arriving.

stats.label: wanted recipes based on what's already in their fridge
found AI diet-tailored suggestions very helpful
said visual appeal directly drove their decision to try a dish
expected to use an AI recipe app at least weekly

data.note: All figures from primary quantitative survey (20+ respondents) and 6 in-depth interviews conducted during the research sprint. These are self-reported preferences, not observed behaviour — stated intent figures should be treated as directional, not predictive.

data.title: Three things users couldn't stop talking about.

items: Tired of cooking the same recipes every week
No time to plan — meal planning feels like another task
Overwhelmed in the kitchen, need something that simplifies
Picky eater with specific taste preferences
Busy traveler who wants to keep up with new cuisines
Hate throwing away ingredients before using them
Wasting money on groceries that expire unused
No system for tracking what's in the fridge
No system to organize saved recipes
Recipes scattered across apps, screenshots, bookmarks
Too much time searching online for something already seen
Wish for a personalized, searchable recipe book

creative_direction.notes: This is the pivot — the case study spine. Each card is a contradiction that shaped every design decision.

data.eyebrow: What the research kept returning to

items.headline: Users wanted AI to decide — but not feel like it was guessing
items.body: Every interview surfaced the same paradox: people wanted AI to take the decision off their plate, but any hint of arbitrariness collapsed trust instantly. One confusing suggestion — a recipe with an ingredient they'd never heard of — was enough to make the whole system feel unreliable. The design challenge wasn't accuracy. It was legibility: making the AI's reasoning feel considered, not random.

items.headline: The real entry point is the fridge, not the search bar
items.body: The existing mental model for recipe apps is browse → save → cook. But the actual 6pm problem is: 'I have these 7 things. What can I make right now?' That's not a search query — it's a constraint. Reframing the entry point from recipe-search to ingredient-input changed what the entire product was trying to solve, and immediately separated Stellar from every existing competitor.

items.headline: First-session trust was the entire product problem
items.body: Users who received one confusing suggestion in their first session assumed the whole system was unreliable. There was no recovery path — no explanation of why the AI suggested what it did, no way to correct it, no signal that the next result would be better. The first session wasn't just important for retention. It was the only session that mattered for trust.

data.text: How might we develop a recipe generator that creates new recipes based on users' favorite ingredients?

data.title: The person the design had to earn.

items.name: Maya
items.biography: Spends her day making decisions — design, product, people. By 6:40pm she has nothing left for 'what should I cook?' She has a rough sense of what's in her fridge and a 30-minute window before she orders delivery.
items.goal: Know what she can cook from what's already in her kitchen in under two minutes — no browsing, no planning, no extra trip to the store.
items.needs: Ingredient-first entry so she starts with what she has, not what she's searching for. Ingredient match score on result cards so she can see 'you have 8/9 ingredients' before tapping in [ingredient match score]. AI reasoning visible on the suggested recipe — not just the name, but why it fits her history ('Mediterranean, 25 min or less, uses fridge staples') so it doesn't feel arbitrary [AI reasoning display].
items.frustrations: Opens a recipe app, searches 'chicken', gets 847 results. Filters by dietary preference, still 200 results. Spends 8 minutes browsing. Orders delivery. Tomorrow she tries again.
items.why_use: Maya selects 6 ingredients from her counter. The top result shows '7/9 ingredients matched' — she checks which 2 she's missing and decides to substitute. She taps the card, sees the reasoning ('matches your cooking history: Mediterranean styles, under 30 minutes'), and trusts it. She didn't browse. She didn't decide. She cooked.

creative_direction.notes: Four decisions built around the trust paradox — each one a different angle on the same problem.

data.headline: Four bets on how to make AI feel trustworthy, not magical.

items.title: Ingredient-first input over recipe-first search
items.description: Problem: users who search 'pasta recipe' are in competition with Google. The insight from research was that the actual problem — 'what can I make from these 6 things?' — isn't a search query, it's a constraint. Options: (A) text search as primary entry — familiar, fast for users with a specific dish in mind. (B) ingredient selection as the main input — forces the app to work from constraint, not from intent. Decision: B. The tradeoff is real: users who arrive wanting to cook a specific dish they've been thinking about all day can't use the primary flow for that — they have to find a secondary search path. The app optimised for the fridge-first use case and accepted that it would be secondary for the recipe-specific one.

items.title: Ingredient match score on result cards before opening
items.description: Problem: users opened a recipe only to find they were missing 4 core ingredients. The qualifying criterion — 'can I actually make this?' — came after the click, not before it. Options: (A) show the recipe title and image on the card; reveal ingredients inside. (B) show ingredient match % on the card surface ('You have 7/9 ingredients'). Decision: B — the match score is what makes the card scannable. Tradeoff: the % creates a false hierarchy. A recipe that uses 4 specialty ingredients you already have might be far better than one scoring 9/12 on generic pantry staples. Users learned to over-rely on the number as a proxy for effort, not for quality. That's a product literacy problem we never fully solved in this sprint.

items.title: AI reasoning visible on suggestion cards
items.description: Problem: users described AI suggestions as 'random' — not because they were wrong, but because there was no signal showing why the AI chose this. Without a reason, every result felt like a guess. Options: (A) show result only — recipe name, image, time — clean, no qualification. (B) add a reasoning line per card: 'Matches your cooking history: Mediterranean, under 30 min, uses pantry staples.' Decision: B. The reasoning line was the single highest-trust signal in prototype testing. Tradeoff: the reasoning is based on behavioral history — in session 1, when history is thin, the reasoning is generic and unconvincing. The feature that most builds trust is weakest precisely when trust matters most.

items.title: Expert escalation as trust fallback, not a bonus feature
items.description: Problem: users who didn't trust AI suggestions had nowhere to go. They could accept a recommendation they doubted, edit the recipe themselves (not a cooking app use case), or leave. Options: (A) let users modify AI-generated recipes directly — they become the authority when AI falls short. (B) offer escalation to a pool of culinary experts who could answer specific questions ('Can I substitute the sumac with something else?'). Decision: B. The expert path isn't a feature — it's a trust recovery mechanism. The tradeoff: the UX is only as good as the experts behind it. An empty or slow expert pool at the wrong moment makes the AI failure worse, not better. Building the escalation path only makes sense if the expert community is there before the feature is.

data.headline: Pick what's in your fridge.
data.description: This is the core of the app. Not a filter. Not a search. Just: what do you have, and what can you make right now?

creative_direction.notes: Slides from right — conversation enters the frame. The chat-first design comes to meet the reader.

data.headline: AI as a cooking companion, not just a search engine.
data.body: The chat experience lets users ask questions, explore recipes, and get cooking advice in real time. When the AI isn't enough, users can escalate to a pool of culinary experts. Conversations worth sharing can be surfaced to the wider Stellar community — turning individual discovery into collective knowledge.
highlights: Chat-first interface — ask anything cooking-related, get instant context
Expert escalation — route to real culinary professionals when needed
Community sharing — useful conversations benefit the whole user base

creative_direction.notes: Simple fade_up, no theatrics — this section is honest, not performative. The learning stands on its own.

data.headline: The concept is sound. The hardest problem wasn't solved.
data.body:
Stellar was a self-initiated design sprint, not a shipped product. The four design decisions above are hypotheses tested in prototype — not production-validated outcomes. In prototype sessions, the ingredient match score and AI reasoning line were the highest-trust signals. Users who saw the reasoning ('matches your cooking history') opened recipes at twice the rate of users who saw the card without it.

What wasn't solved: the cold-start problem. Decision 03 — AI reasoning visible on cards — is weakest exactly when trust matters most: session 1, before behavioral history exists. The feature that most builds trust requires the most data to work. Every AI product I've worked on since has started from this tension: the system is most legible when it knows the user, and most opaque when it needs to earn them.

data.text: The best cooking app isn't the one with the most recipes. It's the one that makes you forget you were ever stuck.
